    • data/processed/
    • data/reference/

Reproducible Project Structure

A simple, consistent way to organise bioinformatics work from day 1

Why Consistency Matters

A consistent project structure makes work easier to understand, review, share, and continue. It reduces the time spent guessing where files live or which script produced which result.

This follows the same general logic used in our GitHub reproducibility session and the GIN-tonic approach: keep projects intentional, keep data handling explicit, and separate raw inputs from derived outputs and working code.

A Simple Standard Layout

project_name/
├── README.md
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── processed/
│   └── reference/
├── scripts/
├── results/
├── docs/
└── env/

You can extend this if needed, but the default layout should stay simple and predictable.

What Belongs in Each Folder

README.md

The project overview. State the purpose of the repository, who maintains it, where the data lives, and how the repository is organised.

data/raw/

Original input data. Treat this as read-only. Do not manually edit files here.

data/processed/

Derived data created from raw inputs. Anything here should be reproducible from documented steps.

data/reference/

Reference genomes, annotations, or other static resources needed for analysis.

scripts/

Analysis code, helper scripts, notebooks that are part of the actual workflow, and small reusable utilities.

results/

Generated outputs such as tables, figures, reports, and summary files.

docs/

Supporting project documentation such as methods notes, data dictionaries, decision logs, or handover notes.

env/

Environment and configuration information such as environment.yml, package lists, container definitions, or config files.

Practical Rules

  • keep raw data separate from processed data
  • keep code separate from outputs
  • give folders names that reflect their purpose rather than the software used
  • avoid scattering key files across the repository root
  • document any departures from the standard structure in the README

Why This Helps

This kind of structure improves:

  • reproducibility, because inputs, code, and outputs are clearly separated
  • onboarding, because new starters can navigate a repository quickly
  • collaboration, because other people can understand where to add or find material
  • continuity, because the project does not depend on one person’s memory

A Slightly Expanded Example

project_name/
├── README.md
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── processed/
│   └── reference/
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_qc.sh
│   ├── 02_alignment.sh
│   └── 03_summary.R
├── results/
│   ├── qc/
│   ├── tables/
│   └── figures/
├── docs/
│   ├── methods.md
│   └── data-location.md
└── env/
    ├── environment.yml
    └── config.yml

Relationship to GitHub

Not every file in a project belongs in GitHub, especially large or controlled data. The important thing is that the repository explains:

  • what data was used
  • where that data lives
  • what scripts were run
  • where outputs are written
  • what someone else would need in order to rerun or extend the work

Start Simple

The goal is not to create a perfect structure up front. The goal is to start with a sensible default and avoid avoidable chaos.

If you are beginning a new repository, use Start Here first and then apply this structure immediately.

Source Code
---
title: "Reproducible Project Structure"
subtitle: "A simple, consistent way to organise bioinformatics work from day 1"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
---

## Why Consistency Matters

A consistent project structure makes work easier to understand, review, share, and continue. It reduces the time spent guessing where files live or which script produced which result.

This follows the same general logic used in our GitHub reproducibility session and the GIN-tonic approach: keep projects intentional, keep data handling explicit, and separate raw inputs from derived outputs and working code.

## A Simple Standard Layout

```text
project_name/
├── README.md
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── processed/
│   └── reference/
├── scripts/
├── results/
├── docs/
└── env/
```

You can extend this if needed, but the default layout should stay simple and predictable.

## What Belongs in Each Folder

### `README.md`

The project overview. State the purpose of the repository, who maintains it, where the data lives, and how the repository is organised.

### `data/raw/`

Original input data. Treat this as read-only. Do not manually edit files here.

### `data/processed/`

Derived data created from raw inputs. Anything here should be reproducible from documented steps.

### `data/reference/`

Reference genomes, annotations, or other static resources needed for analysis.

### `scripts/`

Analysis code, helper scripts, notebooks that are part of the actual workflow, and small reusable utilities.

### `results/`

Generated outputs such as tables, figures, reports, and summary files.

### `docs/`

Supporting project documentation such as methods notes, data dictionaries, decision logs, or handover notes.

### `env/`

Environment and configuration information such as `environment.yml`, package lists, container definitions, or config files.

## Practical Rules

- keep raw data separate from processed data
- keep code separate from outputs
- give folders names that reflect their purpose rather than the software used
- avoid scattering key files across the repository root
- document any departures from the standard structure in the README

## Why This Helps

This kind of structure improves:

- reproducibility, because inputs, code, and outputs are clearly separated
- onboarding, because new starters can navigate a repository quickly
- collaboration, because other people can understand where to add or find material
- continuity, because the project does not depend on one person's memory

## A Slightly Expanded Example

```text
project_name/
├── README.md
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── processed/
│   └── reference/
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_qc.sh
│   ├── 02_alignment.sh
│   └── 03_summary.R
├── results/
│   ├── qc/
│   ├── tables/
│   └── figures/
├── docs/
│   ├── methods.md
│   └── data-location.md
└── env/
    ├── environment.yml
    └── config.yml
```

## Relationship to GitHub

Not every file in a project belongs in GitHub, especially large or controlled data. The important thing is that the repository explains:

- what data was used
- where that data lives
- what scripts were run
- where outputs are written
- what someone else would need in order to rerun or extend the work

## Start Simple

The goal is not to create a perfect structure up front. The goal is to start with a sensible default and avoid avoidable chaos.

If you are beginning a new repository, use [Start Here](start-here.qmd) first and then apply this structure immediately.

UK Dementia Research Institute

 

  • UKDRI
